subject: English
title: Grammar and Structure
outline: Noun, adjectival and adverbial phrases and clauses
Rules of concord (grammatical, proximity, notional, mathematical)

modules.title: Phrases and clauses
modules.html:
A phrase is a group of related words without a subject-verb pair. A clause has both a subject and a finite verb. Phrases and clauses are classified by the job they do — like a noun, an adjective or an adverb.

1. Noun phrase / noun clause

  • Noun phrase: The tall boy in blue won the race. (subject)
  • Noun clause: That he came late annoyed the teacher. (subject)

2. Adjectival (adjective) phrase / clause

  • Adjectival phrase (modifies a noun): The book on the table is mine.
  • Adjective clause (relative): The girl who won the prize is my sister.

3. Adverbial phrase / clause

  • Adverbial phrase: She spoke in a loud voice. (manner)
  • Adverbial clause: I stayed at home because it was raining. (reason)

Common adverbial clause types: time (when, before), reason (because, since), condition (if, unless), concession (although, though), purpose (so that), result (so…that, so that), comparison (than, as…as).

examples.q: Identify the function of the underlined clause: The man who spoke first is the chairman.

modules.title: Concord (subject–verb agreement)
modules.html:
Concord is the grammatical agreement between subject and verb (also pronoun and antecedent).

Common rules

  1. Singular subject → singular verb; plural subject → plural verb. The girl sings. The girls sing.
  2. Two subjects joined by and take a plural verb: Ada and Musa are here.
  3. Two singular subjects joined by or / nor / either…or / neither…nor take a singular verb; if one is plural, the verb agrees with the nearer subject (rule of proximity): Neither the teacher nor the students are ready.
  4. Collective nouns take a singular verb when the group acts together, plural when members act individually.
  5. Indefinite pronouns like each, either, neither, everyone, somebody, nothing take a singular verb.
  6. Notional concord: certain words are singular in form but plural in sense (or vice versa) — The police are…; Mathematics is….
  7. Mathematical concord: Two and two is four (treated as a single sum).

examples.q: Correct or wrong? Every one of the boys are present.

modules.title: Verb tenses — perfect and perfect continuous
modules.html:
The perfect tenses use have/has/had + past participle to link an action to another time. The perfect continuous tenses use have/has/had + been + -ing to emphasise duration.

TenseFormExample
Present perfecthave/has + past participleShe has written the letter.
Past perfecthad + past participleShe had written before I arrived.
Future perfectwill have + past participleShe will have written by noon.
Present perfect continuoushave/has been + -ingShe has been writing for two hours.
Past perfect continuoushad been + -ingShe had been writing when the light went out.
Future perfect continuouswill have been + -ingBy noon she will have been writing for three hours.

examples.q: Complete: By next month I ______ (finish) the project.

modules.title: Adverbs — types, position, comparison
modules.html:
An adverb modifies a verb, adjective, another adverb or a whole sentence.

Types

  • Manner: quickly. Place: here. Time: yesterday. Frequency: often. Degree: very. Focusing: only, even. Sentence: fortunately.

Position

  • Adverbs of frequency go before the main verb: She often visits her aunt; but after the verb be: She is often late.
  • Adverbs of manner usually go after the object: He spoke to us politely.

Comparison

  • Most short adverbs: fast → faster → fastest.
  • Most -ly adverbs: carefully → more carefully → most carefully.
  • Irregular: well → better → best; badly → worse → worst; little → less → least; much → more → most.

quiz.q: A clause must contain:
options: a subject and a finite verb

quiz.q: Because it was raining is a(n):
Identify the noun clause: What she said surprised everyone.
quiz.solution: 'What she said' functions as the subject.

quiz.q: The girl who won the prize is my sister. The underlined clause is:
Choose the correct verb: Neither the teacher nor the students _____ ready.
Each of the students _____ a book.
The jury _____ divided in its opinion.
quiz.solution: Collective noun acting as a body — singular.

quiz.q: The jury _____ arguing among themselves.
Mathematics _____ my favourite subject.
The police _____ investigating the case.
Twenty naira _____ a small amount.
quiz.solution: Money as a sum takes singular verb.

quiz.q: Ten kilometres _____ a long distance.
Perfect tense uses:
She has written the letter. Tense is:
They had left before I arrived. Tense is:
By 8 o'clock, we will have finished. Tense is:
She has been working since 6 a.m. Tense is:
He had been reading for two hours when the light went out. Tense is:
Choose correct: Ada as well as her friends _____ present.
quiz.solution: As well as does not add to the subject — singular.

quiz.q: Choose correct: The number of applicants _____ growing.
Choose correct: A number of students _____ absent.
None of the answers _____ correct.
quiz.solution: With plural noun after of, none takes plural verb.

quiz.q: Bread and butter _____ my breakfast.
quiz.solution: Compound noun treated as one item — singular.

quiz.q: Two and two _____ four.
Position the adverb: (often) — She (often) visits her aunt.
quiz.solution: Adverbs of frequency go before the main verb.

quiz.q: Position the adverb: (often) — She is (often) late.
Comparative of well:
Superlative of little:
Comparative of carefully:
Which is a sentence adverb?
Which is an adverb of degree?
Which is an adverb of frequency?
Adverbial clause of condition begins with:
Adverbial clause of concession begins with:
Adverbial clause of purpose begins with:
Which sentence contains a noun clause?
options: The book on the table is mine.
I know that he is honest.
quiz.solution: That he is honest is the object noun clause.

quiz.q: Which sentence contains an adjective clause?
options: The house that I bought is old.

quiz.q: Which is an adjectival phrase?
Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly?
options: After she leaves, she had lunch.
She had left before I arrived.

quiz.q: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly?
quiz.solution: Present perfect with already — no exact past time.

quiz.q: Neither of them _____ willing to help.
The scissors _____ on the table.
Which sentence follows the rule of proximity?
options: Either you or he are wrong.
Either you or he is wrong.
Either you or he am wrong.
Either you or he were wrong.
quiz.solution: Verb agrees with he — the nearer subject.

quiz.q: The team _____ won its match.
quiz.solution: Team acting as a unit — singular.

quiz.q: My family _____ having breakfast when I called.
Which sentence is correct?
quiz.solution: Adverb of frequency before main verb.

quiz.q: Which of these adverbs shows manner?
Choose correct: She sings _____ than her brother.
Choose the sentence with correct concord.
options: The set of books are new.
The set of books is new.
The sets of books is new.
The set of books were new.
quiz.solution: Set is singular; of books is a prepositional phrase.

quiz.q: Choose the sentence with correct concord.
options: Each boy and girl have a seat.
Each boy and girl has a seat.
Each boy and girl are seated.
Each boy and girl were seated.
